title: Search in BST

content.problemStatement:
Given a binary search tree and an integer **`X`**, search if **`X`** exists in the BST or not.

(**Note:** A binary search tree is a rooted binary tree data structure with the key of each internal node being greater than all the keys in the respective node's left subtree and less than the ones in its right subtree.)

For eg., the given tree is a binary search tree and we can easily search whether node 666 exists in it or not.



**Function Declaration****Function Name**searchInBSTsearchInBSTsearchInBST – Searches for a given value in a Binary Search Tree (BST).

**Parameters**
rootrootroot : A pointer to the root node of the Binary Search Tree.
xxx : An integer value that needs to be searched in the BST.
**Return Value**

Returns a **boolean value**:



truetruetrue if the value xxx exists in the BST.
falsefalsefalse otherwise.



Input Format

The first line of the input contains two space separated integer NNN and XXX — the number of nodes in the binary search tree and the node which we need to search.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on a single line `YES` if XXX is present in the given BST, otherwise `NO`.
constraints: All pi and ci are distinct
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers N and X — the number of nodes in the BST and the value to search. The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers pi, ci and a character Ri ('L' or 'R'), describing an edge where ci is the left (L) or right (R) child of pi.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if X exists in the BST, otherwise "NO".

solution.approach: The BST property is not required to answer the existence query – we only need to know whether X appears among any node values. While reading the edges we can simply check if either the parent value pi or the child value ci equals X. If we ever see X we mark it as found. After processing all edges of a test case we output YES if X was found, otherwise NO. This runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) for the whole input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space (only a few variables).
